input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system is a lab-scale gantry crane consisting of a physical crane and its controller, operating within an industrial automation domain. Key characteristics include being a publicly available, open-source cyber-physical system used for validating digital twin services such as continuous validation, fault detection, and system evolution notation (DarTwin).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides services including optimal control for the physical crane, historical data logging for operational data, real-time data visualization, continuous validation against physical system behavior, and fault detection mechanisms. These services target researchers and educators for validating digital twin methodologies and educational purposes.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation leverages Autodesk Fusion360 for CAD modeling and Capella for system architecture design. The gantrylib Python module packages C4 Containers for modular software structure. Data storage uses TimescaleDB, with visualization through Grafana dashboards. Eclipse Mosquitto serves as the MQTT broker for real-time communication between physical and digital components.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system employs continuous synchronization via a Python Logging Application that records the gantry crane's state in real-time to a database and a Continuous Validation Service that compares sensor measurements to ensure data integrity and temporal consistency.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The architecture is visualized using the C4 Model with context and container diagrams, employing containerization and virtualization technologies for deployment in an ad-hoc approach without a commercial digital twin framework.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The system integrates with external components using MQTT protocol via Eclipse Mosquitto as the message broker for real-time data exchange between physical sensors and digital components.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin incorporates CAD models for geometric representation and a kinematic model for motion simulation. Key services include the Trajectory Generator Service, which computes paths using the kinematic model, and the Simulation Service, which dynamically models crane behavior for real-time analysis and planning.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Two motors with associated controllers, one controlling cart movement and another controlling hoisting movement, and an electromagnet at the hoist end for container pickup.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors include a Swing Angle Encoder measuring the oscillation angle of the hoist during movement and an Anemometer monitoring wind speed to assess environmental impacts on crane stability. These sensors provide real-time data with high temporal resolution for dynamic system monitoring.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: A Continuous Validation Service compares real-time measurements of executed trajectories with simulation outputs using distance metrics against an acceptable threshold.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data flows from the Physical Crane through the Physical Crane Interface (Python program) to the Logging Application, which continuously logs the crane’s state to the TimescaleDB database. The MQTT Broker facilitates signaling between services.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The Trajectory Generator Service generates movement trajectories that are executed by the Control Application via the Physical Crane Interface to control the physical crane hardware.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Services communicate via MQTT protocol using an MQTT Broker. The Physical Crane Interface connects to the crane controllers using a Python program. No specific network topology, bandwidth, or latency details are provided in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The entire system is deployed on a single on-premise personal computer, with TimescaleDB (database), Eclipse Mosquitto (MQTT broker), and Grafana (dashboard) containerized using Docker. All services run within the same host environment, leveraging Docker for isolation and resource management. No cloud or edge deployment is used, and scalability is constrained by the host machine's resources.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Ad-hoc development methodology with GitHub version control; system architecture visualized using the C4 Model; containerization and virtualization technologies for deployment.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Air Quality Management exemplar delivers decision support through runtime visualization for real-time indoor air quality monitoring, simulation for scenario analysis, and predictive modeling to inform virus spread prevention strategies in construction environments.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Lab Scale Gantry Crane employs the C4 Model for software architecture visualization, adhering to its standardized context and container diagram specifications, but adopts an ad-hoc approach without compliance to specific digital twin industry standards.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the physical system as a lab-scale gantry crane and controller, noting its open-source nature, cyber-physical classification, and specific research applications. It is highly specific to the document. Quotes: 'lab-scale gantry crane... physical side consists of the physical crane and its controller', 'It is publicly available... open-source', 'typical Cyber-Physical System', 'demonstrator for the following digital twin related research: continuous validation methods..., fault detection methods and DarTwin'.

The description correctly lists the key services (optimal control, logging, visualization, continuous validation, fault detection) and identifies the target users (researchers and educators). It is detailed and well-supported. Quotes: 'provides services for optimal control, historical data logging, data visualization and continuous validation', 'Dashboard: a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data', 'Logging Application: a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state'.

The description precisely lists the specific tools and frameworks used, assigning them the correct roles as described in the implementation and repository organization sections. Evidence includes: 'CAD models of the crane made in Autodesk Fusion360', 'Arcadia models made in Capella', 'bundled all the Python C4 Containers in a Python module named gantrylib', and the use of 'TimescaleDB', 'Grafana', and 'Eclipse Mosquitto'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the Logging Application and Continuous Validation Service as key components for synchronization and data integrity. It captures the 'continuous' nature of logging and validation mentioned in the text. However, it lacks specific technical details regarding latency, exact frequencies, or temporal granularity requirements which are not detailed in the source but the description could be interpreted as implying a real-time constraint ('real-time' is used in the extraction but 'continuous' is in the source). The source supports 'Logging Application: a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state' and 'Continuous Validation Service: a service that continuously compares measurements'. While 'real-time' is a slight inference from 'continuously logs' and the context of a Cyber-Physical System, it is a reasonable interpretation of synchronization. The score reflects the accuracy of the components identified but notes the lack of specific timing metrics available in the text.

States 'Not in Document', and the source documents do not contain relevant information regarding multiple digital twin instances, hierarchical structures, or coordination mechanisms between twins. The text describes a single system. Quote: 'The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a digital counterpart', 'The digital twin consists of six containers'.

Accurately describes the architecture visualization using C4 models, the use of containerization, and the ad-hoc approach without commercial frameworks. Supported by quotes: 'we use the C4 Model... to visualize and describe the architecture of the system with a context and container diagram', 'In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework'.

The extraction accurately describes the integration mechanism. The text explicitly mentions 'MQTT Broker: an MQTT broker for signaling between services' and identifies the specific software as 'Eclipse Mosquitto' ('the ubiquitous and open-source Eclipse Mosquitto was used'). The extraction mentions 'physical sensors' which aligns with the description of the physical crane components (Swing Angle Encoder, Anemometer) sending data to the digital side. The description of real-time data exchange is supported by the context of continuous logging and validation.

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the CAD models and kinematic model used in the digital twin. It correctly describes the specific roles of the Trajectory Generator Service (computing paths via kinematic models) and the Simulation Service (modeling crane behavior), all of which are explicitly detailed in the source text. Quotes: 'digital twin containing CAD models', 'Trajectory Generator Service: a service that utilizes a kinematics model of the crane', 'Simulation Service: a service that implements a simulation of the crane'.

The extraction accurately lists the motors, controllers, and their specific functions (cart and hoisting movement), as well as the electromagnet for pickup. It matches the source description almost verbatim. Supported by: 'Two Motors and associated Controllers, one to control the cart’s movement, one to control the hoisting movement. An Electromagnet at the end of the hoist to pick up containers'

The extraction correctly identifies the Swing Angle Encoder and Anemometer and their respective measurements. It aligns well with the source, though it adds generic descriptors like 'high temporal resolution' not explicitly found in the text. Quotes: 'a Swing Angle Encoder measures the swinging motion of the hoist', 'an Anemometer measures the wind speed'.

The extraction precisely describes the Continuous Validation Service, its function of comparing measurements with simulations, and the use of distance metrics and thresholds. This is a direct match with the source. Supported by: 'Continuous Validation Service: a service that continuously compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics between them and compares the value with an acceptable threshold.'

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

It is specific to the system but does not elaborate on specific data types or transmission frequencies, which are not explicitly detailed in the source.
quotes: Physical Crane Interface: the interface to the physical crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the crane.
Logging Application: a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
MQTT Broker: an MQTT broker for signaling between services.

block_4_judge.reasoning: Correctly identifies the components involved in controlling the physical crane: the Trajectory Generator Service, Control Application, and Physical Crane Interface. The extraction synthesizes these components into a coherent control flow described in the architecture section.
quotes: Trajectory Generator Service: a service that utilizes a kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement trajectories for the crane.
Control Application: a Python application that uses the crane interface to control the crane.

block_4_judge.reasoning: Provides a precise summary of the communication protocols and interfaces (MQTT for services, Python for hardware) found in the text. It correctly identifies that the document does not provide details on network topology or bandwidth.
quotes: MQTT Broker: an MQTT broker for signaling between services.
Physical Crane Interface: ... a Python program connecting to the controllers of the crane.

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction is highly accurate and specific to the source text. It correctly identifies the deployment on a single personal computer, the specific use of Docker for the database, broker, and dashboard, and names the correct technologies (TimescaleDB, Eclipse Mosquitto, Grafana). The inference about no cloud usage is well-supported by the description of the local setup.

block_5_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.
